title: On Computable Numbers, with an Application to the Entscheidungsproblem
why: Defined computation itself — the Turing machine, and the limits of what any machine can compute.
venue: Proceedings of the London Mathematical Society

title: A Mathematical Theory of Communication
why: Founded information theory and gave us the bit — the basis of all digital communication and storage.

title: Computing Machinery and Intelligence
why: Posed the question 'can machines think?' and framed it as the imitation game (the Turing test).

title: A Note on Two Problems in Connexion with Graphs
why: Dijkstra's shortest-path algorithm — still routing the internet and your GPS today.

title: An Algorithm for the Machine Calculation of Complex Fourier Series
why: The Fast Fourier Transform — made digital signal processing practical across all of engineering.

title: Go To Statement Considered Harmful
why: Launched structured programming and the discipline of writing comprehensible software.

title: A Relational Model of Data for Large Shared Data Banks
why: Invented the relational database — the foundation of essentially every business system since.

title: The Complexity of Theorem-Proving Procedures
why: Defined NP-completeness — the line between tractable and intractable problems (P vs NP).

title: Reducibility Among Combinatorial Problems
why: Showed 21 classic problems are NP-complete, mapping the landscape of computational hardness.

title: A Protocol for Packet Network Intercommunication
why: TCP/IP — the protocol that became the Internet.

title: New Directions in Cryptography
why: Invented public-key cryptography — how strangers exchange secrets without meeting.

title: A Method for Obtaining Digital Signatures and Public-Key Cryptosystems
why: RSA — the public-key cryptosystem that secures the web.

title: Time, Clocks, and the Ordering of Events in a Distributed System
why: Logical clocks — the conceptual foundation of distributed systems.

title: How to Share a Secret
why: Secret sharing — split a secret so any k of n parts reconstruct it, fewer reveal nothing.

title: The Byzantine Generals Problem
why: Defined fault tolerance against arbitrary/malicious failures — later the heart of blockchains.
venue: ACM Transactions on Programming Languages and Systems

title: A Theory of the Learnable
why: PAC learning — gave machine learning a rigorous theoretical foundation.

title: Impossibility of Distributed Consensus with One Faulty Process
why: The FLP impossibility — a hard limit every consensus protocol since has had to route around.

title: Learning representations by back-propagating errors
why: Backpropagation — the algorithm that trains essentially every neural network.

title: Information Management: A Proposal
why: The blueprint for the World Wide Web.
venue: CERN Document Server (European Organization for Nuclear Research)

title: The Anatomy of a Large-Scale Hypertextual Web Search Engine
why: PageRank and the architecture of Google — organized the web.

title: The Part-Time Parliament
why: Paxos — the consensus algorithm running the world's databases and cloud control planes.

title: Gradient-Based Learning Applied to Document Recognition
why: Convolutional neural networks (LeNet) — the template for modern computer vision.

title: Random Forests
why: A robust, general-purpose ML method that dominated tabular prediction for a decade.

title: MapReduce: Simplified Data Processing on Large Clusters
why: Made big-data processing across thousands of machines simple — launched the data era.

title: A Fast Learning Algorithm for Deep Belief Nets
why: Reignited deep learning and showed deep networks could be trained.

title: Bitcoin: A Peer-to-Peer Electronic Cash System
why: Solved decentralized consensus among untrusting strangers — blockchain and a new asset class.

title: ImageNet Classification with Deep Convolutional Neural Networks
why: AlexNet — the result that started the deep-learning revolution.

title: Efficient Estimation of Word Representations in Vector Space
why: word2vec — learned word embeddings, the idea that meaning can be geometry.

title: Generative Adversarial Networks
why: GANs — two networks competing to generate realistic data; launched modern generative AI.

title: Sequence to Sequence Learning with Neural Networks
why: Seq2seq — neural networks that map sequences to sequences; the basis of neural translation.

title: Adam: A Method for Stochastic Optimization
why: Adam — the default optimizer that trains most deep-learning models.

title: Deep Residual Learning for Image Recognition
why: ResNet — residual connections let networks go hundreds of layers deep.

title: Attention Is All You Need
why: The Transformer — the architecture behind every modern large language model, including this one.

title: BERT: Pre-training of Deep Bidirectional Transformers for Language Understanding
why: Pretrained language models — fine-tune one big model for everything; reshaped NLP.
venue: DROPS (Schloss Dagstuhl – Leibniz Center for Informatics)

title: Language Models are Few-Shot Learners
why: GPT-3 — showed that scale alone yields in-context learning; the template for modern AI.

title: Highly accurate protein structure prediction with AlphaFold
why: AI solved a 50-year grand-challenge in biology — protein structure prediction.
